summary: Fowler argues that companies make a fundamental mistake when they set out to 'build a mobile application' as a separate project. Instead, organizations should think about building a single transmedia application that presents itself across multiple devices—phone, tablet, desktop, TV—each adapted to its usage context. The user experience should be seamless across these presentations, allowing users to switch between devices naturally. This isn't new thinking; it's basic systems analysis applied to the modern multi-device world, where software must meet users wherever they are.
key_points: Thinking of mobile, web, and tablet as separate applications is a fundamental framing error—they should be different presentations of one unified application
Each device is used in different contexts with different needs, so the experience must be designed with those contexts in mind while maintaining coherence
The iPod's success came from being 'software above the level of a single device'—seamlessly connecting handheld, web backend, and PC as one product
Transmedia thinking must permeate all aspects of the project: user experience, software architecture, project management—not just development
This approach is essentially traditional systems analysis: understanding the domain process and how software can best support it across all touchpoints
money_quotes: Even though you want users to interact with a mobile device, you should never think of building a mobile application.
It's as though the single screens of yesterday have been shattered and the shards have been embedded into our pockets, our environment, and the products we use.
The fact that the company commissioning the software wants to send out the web site and mobile app to different software houses is less relevant than 1923's baseball scores.
I consider the desire to create a mobile application to be a Wrong Thought.
You need to start with the user, figuring out what it is that they want to do and how software can help them do that.
The application, and the project team that builds it, need to be thought of as a single whole, all dedicated to making their users happy.
key_insight: Don't build a mobile app—build one application with multiple device-appropriate presentations, designed from the user's perspective rather than the technology's.
